nativeRange.habitat: Coastal reefs and drop-offs exposed to strong tidal currents; depth range approximately 5–30 m. Juveniles tend to occupy deep rubble slopes with invertebrate growth.

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on sponges and tunicates in the wild, with algae and occasional invertebrates also consumed. In captivity, tends to accept Spirulina, marine algae, sponge-enriched angelfish preparations, mysis shrimp, krill, and other meaty fare. Generally advised to offer varied meals multiple times daily; newly acquired specimens have been known to initially refuse prepared foods and may benefit from live brine shrimp or sponge-encrusted rock to trigger feeding.

summary: A striking Indo-Pacific angelfish with a velvety black body, gray-splashed head, and yellow tail; generally considered intermediate in difficulty and has been known to nip at corals and clam mantles.
careNotes:
The Black Velvet Angelfish is generally considered one of the more manageable large angelfishes, though it tends to be shy and sensitive during initial acclimation. Newly introduced specimens have been known to hide and refuse food; a well-established aquarium with ample live rock for grazing and numerous hiding places tends to improve outcomes. Generally advised to keep singly; conspecific and congeneric aggression has been reported, particularly among juveniles.

This species has been documented eating sponges and tunicates in the wild, so sponge-enriched angelfish preparations are broadly recommended once the fish is settled. It is generally considered not reef safe — has been known to nip at stony and soft corals, clam mantles, and tubeworms, particularly if underfed. Susceptible to marine ich when stressed.

Minimum tank size disagreement across sources: LiveAquaria states 125 gal (~473 L), ReefApp states 700 L, AquaticCommunity states 75 gal (~284 L), and TankFacts states 75 gal (~284 L). The spread exceeds 2× (284–700 L), so the averaged value of ~435 L is used but flagged for reviewer verification. The 75-gal figures may be low for an adult at 20 cm.

Temperature cross-check: LiveAquaria gives 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C, captive recommendation); FishBase wild data gives 25.5–29.3°C; TankFacts gives 76–80°F (24.4–26.7°C). Averaged across all three sources: min ~24.0°C, max ~27.2°C, rounded to 24–27°C.

Care level: LiveAquaria and TankFacts rate this species as moderate; ReefApp rates it delicate/advanced. Average position is intermediate.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to be hermaphroditic with no reliable external sex differences between male and female. Breeding in captivity is considered very difficult and has rarely been documented; spawning attempts are not commonly reported in the hobby literature.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (20 cm TL confirmed), taxonomy, native range (Indo-West Pacific: Indo-Malayan region and New Guinea, northward to southern Japan), wild temperature range (25.5–29.3°C), depth range (5–30 m), and diet (sponges and tunicates). Order confirmed as Acanthuriformes.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (20 cm), minimum tank (700 L / 185 gal), care level (delicate), salinity (~1.026), reef-safe (no), temperament, and diet. Tank size of 700 L included in averaging; minimum tank spread exceeded 2× and is flagged for review.

additional.name: AquaticCommunity
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (17 cm), minimum tank (75 gal / 284 L), temperament (semi-aggressive), diet, and native range. Size of 17 cm included in adult-size averaging.

additional.name: TankFacts
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (20 cm), temperature (76–80°F / 24.4–26.7°C), pH (7.8–8.4), salinity (1.023–1.026), minimum tank (75 gal / 284 L), and care level (moderate). Temperature range used in averaging.

fish.breedingNotes: Has been reported to be hermaphroditic with no reliable external sex differences. Breeding in captivity is considered very difficult and has rarely been documented.
